An issue in `import-in-the-middle` allows for Remote Code Execution (RCE) if an application passes user-supplied input directly to an `import()` function. The vulnerability occurs because the module specifier (URL) is interpolated directly into the generated wrapper module's source code as a string without proper escaping. If a malicious specifier containing quotes and newlines is provided, it can break out of the string literal and execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the application.

To fix this, we must properly escape the module specifier when generating the wrapper code. Using `JSON.stringify()` ensures that the URL is safely encoded as a valid JavaScript string literal, neutralizing any attempt to break out of the string context.

An issue with `libxmljs` is that the native C++ bindings do not properly validate the type of the input passed to the parsing functions. When a non-string and non-buffer argument is provided, V8 attempts to implicitly convert it to a string by calling its `.toString()` method. If the provided object has a `.toString` property that is not a function, V8 will crash, leading to a Denial of Service (DoS).

To fix this, we must enforce strict type checking at the native boundary before any V8 string conversions occur. The input must explicitly be either a V8 String or a Node.js Buffer.
